resume:
VP of Learning & Outcomes | 15 years scaling edtech and academic programs

Summary:
Built and led learning organizations across K-12 and higher-ed SaaS. Drove measurable improvements in learner outcomes and retention at national scale.

BrightPath Learning — VP, Learning & Outcomes (2019-present)
- Own the academic efficacy function and the outcomes data team (reported as 40 people in one section, 22 in another)
- Lifted course completion from 54% to 'over 90%' across the platform
- Launched an adaptive-learning engine credited with a '3x improvement in mastery'
- Cut time-to-competency 'roughly in half' for the flagship certification track

ScholarBridge — Director of Curriculum (2014-2019)
- Led a curriculum team (listed as 30, elsewhere as 12)
- Redesigned the assessment framework used across 200 districts

Earlier: Classroom teacher, then instructional designer.

Note: completion-rate baseline appears as both 54% and 61% in different sections; team headcounts conflict between summary and role bullets.
jd: Meridian Education is hiring a VP of Learning & Outcomes to own academic efficacy across our higher-ed platform serving 1.4M active learners. Reporting to the Chief Academic Officer, you will lead a 45-person organization spanning learning science, assessment, and outcomes analytics. You will be accountable for board-reported metrics: verified course-completion rate (currently 58%, target 72% in 18 months), learner-reported satisfaction, and a defensible efficacy methodology that survives external academic peer review. We are under regulatory scrutiny on outcomes claims, so every published number must be traceable to a cohort definition and a measurement window. You will inherit a contested efficacy study and a pending accreditation review. Comfort presenting to the board's education-quality committee is required.

history.q: Start with your current role at BrightPath — what function do you own and how big is the team?
Your resume lists the outcomes team as 40 in one place and 22 in another. Which is it?
You say completion went from 54% to 'over 90%.' What's the exact current number and the cohort it covers?
Elsewhere the baseline reads 61%, not 54%. Why the discrepancy?
Walk me through how completion rate is defined at BrightPath — what counts as a completion?
The adaptive engine 'credited with 3x improvement in mastery' — how was mastery measured before and after?
Was that a controlled comparison, or pre/post on the same population?
Who outside your team validated the efficacy methodology?
'Time-to-competency cut roughly in half' — from what to what, over which track?
Let's talk about a claim that didn't hold up. Tell me about an outcomes number you had to walk back.
How did you handle the contested efficacy study you mentioned earlier?
When the board's quality committee pushed on a number, what did they ask for?
Give me one metric where you can name the cohort, the window, and the source system right now.
What was your personal role in defining that measurement, versus the analytics team's?
If an external reviewer audited your headline completion number tomorrow, what would they find?
I want one number, fully specified — the current completion rate, its cohort, and its measurement window. Can you give me that?

candidate_last_answer: You know, before I answer that — let me ask you something. How does Meridian currently define a completion? Because honestly, I think the question itself is where most organizations go wrong. At BrightPath our numbers are frankly best-in-class, completion is north of 90%, mastery tripled, time-to-competency basically halved — these are category-leading results that speak for themselves. So I'd flip it back to you: what bar are you actually holding the next VP to?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: completion is north of 90%, mastery tripled, time-to-competency basically halved

expected-yield: forces the candidate off the counter-question and onto a single fully-specified completion figure — exact percentage, the cohort/denominator definition, and the measurement window from a named source system — exposing whether the 'north of 90%' claim has a defensible, auditable basis
